text: A user creates a temporary table in Session A and inserts data into it. Session B, logged in as the same user, attempts SELECT * FROM that temporary table. What is the result?

options.A: Session B sees all data inserted by Session A because they are the same user
options.B: Session B receives an error because temporary tables are visible only within the session that created them
options.C: Session B sees the table but it contains zero rows
options.D: Session B sees the table only if both sessions use the same warehouse

explanation: Temporary tables are scoped to the session that created them and are invisible to all other sessions, even by the same user. They are automatically dropped when the session ends. This is distinct from transient tables, which persist across sessions.

text: A developer needs to return multiple rows from a user-defined function that will be used in a FROM clause: SELECT * FROM TABLE(my_func(42)). Which type of function should be created?

options.A: A scalar UDF that returns a VARIANT array
options.B: A User-Defined Table Function (UDTF) that returns a tabular result set
options.C: A stored procedure invoked with CALL
options.D: A scalar UDF with RETURNS TABLE syntax

explanation: A UDTF (User-Defined Table Function) returns a tabular result set and can be used in FROM clauses with the TABLE() wrapper. Scalar UDFs return a single value per row. Stored procedures are invoked with CALL and cannot appear in FROM clauses. RETURNS TABLE is the correct syntax for defining a UDTF.

text: An organization has accounts in AWS US-East, Azure West Europe, and GCP Australia. An admin needs to manage all accounts centrally — creating new accounts, enabling replication, and viewing org-level usage. Which role is required?

options.A: ACCOUNTADMIN in the primary account
options.B: SYSADMIN with cross-account privileges
options.C: ORGADMIN, which operates at the organization level
options.D: SECURITYADMIN with MANAGE GRANTS on all accounts

explanation: ORGADMIN operates at the organization level and can create accounts, enable replication between accounts, and view organization-wide usage. ACCOUNTADMIN is scoped to a single account. There is no cross-account SYSADMIN or SECURITYADMIN capability.

text: A data engineer queries a table and notices that SHOW TABLES reports 'bytes' as 0 even though the table contains data loaded yesterday. The engineer suspects the table was created with CREATE TABLE ... CLONE source_table. What explains the zero bytes?

options.A: The clone failed silently and the table is empty
options.B: Zero-copy cloning means the clone shares micro-partitions with the source — no additional storage is consumed until data diverges
options.C: Storage metrics are delayed by 24 hours in SHOW TABLES
options.D: Cloned tables do not store data; they are read-only pointers

explanation: Zero-copy cloning creates a metadata snapshot pointing to the same micro-partitions as the source. No additional storage is used until data in the clone or source diverges through DML. SHOW TABLES may report 0 bytes for the clone's owned storage until divergence occurs.

text: A warehouse is configured with AUTO_SUSPEND = 300 and AUTO_RESUME = TRUE. A user submits a query 10 minutes after the warehouse last suspended. What happens?

options.A: The query fails because the warehouse is suspended and must be manually resumed
options.B: The warehouse automatically resumes, the query executes, and the warehouse suspends again after 300 seconds of inactivity
options.C: The query is queued until an administrator resumes the warehouse
options.D: The warehouse resumes but the query is rejected due to a cold-start timeout

explanation: With AUTO_RESUME = TRUE, a suspended warehouse automatically resumes when a query is submitted. After the query completes and 300 seconds (5 minutes) of inactivity pass, it auto-suspends again. There is no cold-start timeout that rejects queries — the first query simply takes longer due to SSD cache being empty.

text: Which TWO Cortex AI SQL functions can be called directly in a SELECT statement for text analysis without any model training? (Select TWO)

options.A: CORTEX.COMPLETE — generates text responses using an LLM
options.B: FORECAST — predicts future time-series values
options.C: CORTEX.SENTIMENT — returns sentiment score for input text
options.D: ANOMALY_DETECTION — detects outliers in time-series data
options.E: CORTEX.FINETUNE — trains a custom language model

explanation: CORTEX.COMPLETE and CORTEX.SENTIMENT are Cortex AI SQL functions that work directly in SELECT without training. FORECAST and ANOMALY_DETECTION are ML Functions that require a training step. CORTEX.FINETUNE is for fine-tuning models, not direct text analysis.

text: A Snowflake account on Standard Edition wants to create a multi-cluster warehouse to handle concurrent user workloads. Is this possible?

options.A: Yes — multi-cluster warehouses are available on all editions
options.B: No — multi-cluster warehouses require Enterprise Edition or higher
options.C: Yes — but only with a maximum of 2 clusters on Standard Edition
options.D: No — multi-cluster warehouses require Business Critical Edition or higher

explanation: Multi-cluster warehouses require Enterprise Edition or higher. Standard Edition supports only single-cluster warehouses. There is no limited cluster option for Standard Edition.

text: A table contains a VARIANT column with nested JSON. An engineer creates a view that flattens and casts specific keys into typed columns. Another engineer then creates a materialized view on this flattened view. What happens?

options.A: The materialized view is created successfully on top of the view
options.B: The creation fails because materialized views must reference a single base table directly, not a view
options.C: The materialized view is created but cannot auto-refresh
options.D: The creation succeeds only if the view is a secure view

explanation: Materialized views must be defined directly against a single base table. They cannot reference other views, and they do not support JOINs. To achieve a similar result, use a dynamic table, which supports complex queries including JOINs and views.

text: A developer writes a Python UDF that processes each input row and returns a VARCHAR. The UDF is called in a WHERE clause. Where does the UDF code execute?

options.A: On the developer's local Python runtime
options.B: On the virtual warehouse assigned to the session, inside a secure sandbox
options.C: On a dedicated Snowflake UDF compute pool separate from the warehouse
options.D: On the Cloud Services layer alongside query optimization

explanation: Python UDFs execute on the virtual warehouse's compute nodes inside a secure, sandboxed environment. They are not executed on the client, on a separate pool, or on the Cloud Services layer. This is the same warehouse that runs the SQL query referencing the UDF.

text: A task DAG has root task T1 (SCHEDULE = '5 MINUTE'), child T2 (predecessor = T1), and child T3 (predecessor = T2). An engineer attempts to add a SCHEDULE clause to T2. What happens?

options.A: T2 now runs on its own schedule independently of T1
options.B: The ALTER fails — only the root task in a DAG can have a SCHEDULE; child tasks are triggered by their predecessors
options.C: Both T1's and T2's schedules apply, and T2 runs at whichever fires first
options.D: T2's schedule overrides T1's schedule for the entire DAG

explanation: In a task DAG, only the root task has a SCHEDULE. Child tasks are triggered when their predecessor tasks complete successfully. Attempting to set a SCHEDULE on a non-root task in an existing DAG is invalid. Each DAG has exactly one entry point (the root task's schedule).

text: A network policy is set at the account level allowing IP range 10.0.0.0/16. A separate network policy is applied to a specific user allowing only 192.168.1.0/24. The user connects from 10.0.5.10. Is the connection allowed?

options.A: Yes — the account-level policy permits this IP
options.B: No — the user-level policy overrides the account-level policy, and 10.0.5.10 is not in the user's allowed range
options.C: Yes — both policies are evaluated together and the union of allowed IPs applies
options.D: No — conflicting policies cause all connections to be denied

explanation: User-level network policies OVERRIDE account-level policies (they are not additive). When a user has a specific network policy, only that policy is evaluated. Since 10.0.5.10 is not in 192.168.1.0/24, the connection is denied regardless of the account-level policy.

text: An administrator wants to automatically provision and deprovision Snowflake users based on changes in the company's identity provider (Okta). Which protocol enables this automated lifecycle management?

options.A: SAML 2.0 for federated single sign-on
options.B: SCIM 2.0 for automated user and group provisioning
options.C: OAuth 2.0 for token-based application authorization
options.D: LDAP for direct directory integration

explanation: SCIM (System for Cross-domain Identity Management) 2.0 enables automated provisioning and deprovisioning of users and groups from an identity provider to Snowflake. SAML 2.0 handles SSO authentication but not user lifecycle. OAuth handles application authorization. Snowflake does not support direct LDAP integration.

text: An object tag COST_CENTER = 'ENGINEERING' is applied to a warehouse. A colleague believes this tag automatically restricts which roles can use the warehouse. Is this correct?

options.A: Yes — tags with cost center values automatically restrict usage to matching roles
options.B: No — tags are metadata labels only and do not enforce any access control by themselves
options.C: Yes — but only when combined with a network policy
options.D: No — tags can only be applied to tables and columns, not warehouses

explanation: Tags in Snowflake are metadata labels used for categorization, tracking, and governance reporting. They do NOT enforce access control on their own. To enforce behavior based on tags, you must attach a masking policy or row access policy to the tag. Tags can be applied to most objects including warehouses.

text: An administrator grants FUTURE GRANTS on tables in schema ANALYTICS.REPORTING to ROLE_BI with SELECT privilege. An existing table T_OLD was created before the future grant. Does ROLE_BI have SELECT on T_OLD?

options.A: Yes — future grants retroactively apply to all existing objects
options.B: No — future grants apply only to objects created AFTER the grant is issued; T_OLD requires a separate explicit grant
options.C: Yes — but only after running ALTER SCHEMA ... REFRESH GRANTS
options.D: No — future grants only work at the database level, not schema level

explanation: FUTURE GRANTS apply only to objects created after the grant statement is executed. Pre-existing objects are not affected. To grant SELECT on T_OLD, an explicit GRANT SELECT ON TABLE T_OLD TO ROLE ROLE_BI is required. Future grants work at both database and schema levels.

text: Which TWO statements about resource monitors in Snowflake are TRUE? (Select TWO)

options.A: Resource monitors can track serverless compute credits such as Snowpipe and automatic clustering
options.B: Only ACCOUNTADMIN can create resource monitors — this privilege cannot be delegated
options.C: A warehouse-level monitor and an account-level monitor operate independently, and whichever threshold is hit first takes effect
options.D: When the TRIGGERS clause is altered, new triggers are added to existing ones
options.E: Resource monitors track only user-managed warehouse credits, not serverless compute

explanation: Only ACCOUNTADMIN can CREATE resource monitors (non-delegable). Resource monitors track ONLY user-managed warehouse credits — not serverless (Snowpipe, clustering, MVs). Account and warehouse monitors are independent (first trigger wins). ALTER ... TRIGGERS replaces ALL triggers, not additive. Serverless = use budgets instead.

text: An administrator needs to transfer ownership of all objects in a schema from ROLE_OLD to ROLE_NEW while preserving existing access grants. Which clause accomplishes this?

options.A: GRANT OWNERSHIP ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA ... TO ROLE_NEW REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS
options.B: GRANT OWNERSHIP ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA ... TO ROLE_NEW COPY CURRENT GRANTS
options.C: ALTER SCHEMA ... SET OWNER = ROLE_NEW
options.D: GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA ... TO ROLE_NEW

explanation: COPY CURRENT GRANTS preserves existing privilege grants when transferring ownership. REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS (the default) removes all existing grants during transfer. ALTER SCHEMA SET OWNER is not valid syntax. GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES grants permissions but does not transfer ownership.

text: A projection policy is applied to the SSN column of the EMPLOYEES table. When a role not included in the policy attempts SELECT SSN FROM EMPLOYEES, what happens?

options.A: The SSN values are returned as masked/hashed values
options.B: The query fails with an error because the role is not authorized to project (SELECT) that column
options.C: NULL values are returned for every row
options.D: The column is silently omitted from the result set

explanation: Projection policies control which roles can SELECT (project) a column. Unlike masking policies that return modified values, projection policies block the column entirely — unauthorized roles receive an error when attempting to include the column in their SELECT. This is a stricter form of column-level security.

text: An engineer runs COPY INTO my_table and receives an error: a string value in the CSV exceeds the target VARCHAR(50) column length. The engineer wants to truncate long values instead of erroring. Which parameter should be set?

options.A: ON_ERROR = CONTINUE
options.B: TRUNCATECOLUMNS = TRUE
options.C: FORCE = TRUE
options.D: ERROR_ON_COLUMN_COUNT_MISMATCH = FALSE

explanation: TRUNCATECOLUMNS = TRUE silently truncates string values that exceed the target column length instead of raising an error. ON_ERROR = CONTINUE skips bad rows but does not truncate values. FORCE bypasses dedup. ERROR_ON_COLUMN_COUNT_MISMATCH handles column count differences, not value length.

text: A JSON file on a stage contains an array of objects: [{"id":1,"name":"A"},{"id":2,"name":"B"}]. The engineer wants each array element to become a separate row in the target table. Which file format option enables this?

options.A: MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME = CASE_INSENSITIVE
options.B: STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY = TRUE
options.C: STRIP_NULL_VALUES = TRUE
options.D: FLATTEN_ARRAYS = TRUE

explanation: STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY = TRUE removes the outer [ ] wrapper so each JSON object in the array becomes a separate row during loading. Without it, the entire array is loaded as a single VARIANT value. STRIP_NULL_VALUES converts JSON nulls to SQL NULLs. FLATTEN_ARRAYS is not a valid file format option. MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME handles column name matching.

text: A data pipeline uses COPY INTO with ON_ERROR = CONTINUE to load CSV files from a stage. After loading, the engineer wants to identify which specific rows failed. Which function provides this detail?

options.A: RESULT_SCAN(LAST_QUERY_ID())
options.B: VALIDATE(my_table, JOB_ID => '_last')
options.C: SHOW COPY HISTORY FOR my_table
options.D: SELECT * FROM TABLE(INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COPY_HISTORY(...))

explanation: VALIDATE(table_name, JOB_ID => '_last') returns row-level error details from the most recent COPY INTO operation, including the error message, line number, and character position for each failed row. COPY_HISTORY shows file-level status. RESULT_SCAN shows query results, not load errors.

text: A Snowpipe ingestion job fails on several files. The engineer wants to receive automatic notifications when failures occur. Which Snowpipe feature enables this?

options.A: Setting ON_ERROR = ABORT_STATEMENT on the pipe definition
options.B: Configuring an ERROR_INTEGRATION on the pipe that sends notifications via a notification integration
options.C: Enabling SYSTEM$PIPE_STATUS() polling from a scheduled task
options.D: Setting COPY_OPTIONS = (ERROR_NOTIFICATION = TRUE)

explanation: ERROR_INTEGRATION is a pipe-level property that references a notification integration. When ingestion errors occur, Snowflake sends notifications (email, webhook, or cloud messaging) through the integration. ON_ERROR controls error handling behavior, not notifications. SYSTEM$PIPE_STATUS is manual.

text: A file was loaded via bulk COPY INTO 60 days ago. The engineer runs COPY INTO again for the same file without FORCE = TRUE. What happens?

options.A: The file is loaded again because the metadata expired after 14 days
options.B: The file is skipped because bulk COPY metadata is retained for 64 days and the file is still tracked
options.C: The file is loaded again because deduplication only works within a single COPY statement
options.D: An error is raised because the same file cannot be referenced twice

explanation: Bulk COPY INTO retains load metadata for 64 days. Within this window, Snowflake tracks which files have been loaded and skips them automatically (deduplication). The file at 60 days is still within the 64-day window, so it is skipped. After 64 days, or with FORCE = TRUE, it would be loaded again.

text: A task is created as a serverless task (no WAREHOUSE specified). What determines the initial compute size for the task's execution?

options.A: Snowflake automatically determines the size based on query complexity
options.B: The USER_TASK_MANAGED_INITIAL_WAREHOUSE_SIZE parameter specifies the starting size
options.C: Serverless tasks always run on an XS-equivalent compute
options.D: The size is inherited from the account's default warehouse

explanation: Serverless tasks use Snowflake-managed compute. The USER_TASK_MANAGED_INITIAL_WAREHOUSE_SIZE parameter sets the initial compute size. Snowflake can automatically adjust the size for subsequent runs based on workload, but the initial size is controlled by this parameter.

text: A stream on TABLE_A has not been consumed for a long time. The engineer queries the stream and receives an error indicating the stream is stale. What must be done to restore change tracking?

options.A: Run ALTER STREAM ... REFRESH to reset the offset
options.B: Drop and recreate the stream — stale streams cannot be recovered
options.C: Set MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS to a higher value to un-stale the stream
options.D: Run SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA() to force a metadata refresh

explanation: A stale stream means the offset has fallen behind the available Time Travel data and cannot be recovered. The only solution is to DROP the stream and CREATE a new one. ALTER STREAM REFRESH does not exist. Increasing MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS prevents future staleness but cannot fix an already-stale stream.

text: A COPY INTO command uses ON_ERROR = CONTINUE and PURGE = TRUE. Some files have errors. Which files are deleted from the stage?

options.A: All files are deleted regardless of errors
options.B: Only files that were fully loaded without any errors are deleted
options.C: Files that were processed are deleted — even those that had some rows skipped due to errors
options.D: No files are deleted when ON_ERROR = CONTINUE is used

explanation: With PURGE = TRUE and ON_ERROR = CONTINUE, files that were processed (opened and read) are purged from the stage — even if some rows within them had errors and were skipped. Only files that were entirely skipped (e.g., by SKIP_FILE) are NOT purged. CONTINUE processes all files, so all are purged.

text: Which TWO operations require a running virtual warehouse? (Select TWO)

options.A: Running a COPY INTO command to load data from a stage
options.B: Querying a pipe's status with SYSTEM$PIPE_STATUS()
options.C: Executing a complex JOIN query between two large tables
options.D: Listing files on a stage with LIST @my_stage
options.E: Creating a new database with CREATE DATABASE

explanation: COPY INTO (bulk) and complex queries require a running warehouse for compute. SYSTEM$PIPE_STATUS, LIST, and CREATE DATABASE are metadata/Cloud Services operations that do not require a warehouse. Snowpipe uses serverless compute, but bulk COPY uses the session's warehouse.

text: A recurring dashboard query aggregates sales by region and month. The underlying table has 2 billion rows. The query runs every 15 minutes and always produces the same result until new data is loaded (once daily). Which optimization tool is MOST appropriate?

options.A: Add a clustering key on (region, month)
options.B: Create a materialized view with the aggregation pre-computed
options.C: Enable Query Acceleration Service for the dashboard warehouse
options.D: Increase the warehouse size to XL

explanation: A materialized view pre-computes and caches the aggregation result, and auto-refreshes when base data changes (once daily). Since the query repeats frequently against unchanging data on a single table, an MV eliminates redundant computation. Clustering helps pruning but still requires full aggregation. QAS helps outliers, not repeated stable queries.

text: An analyst examines a Query Profile and sees the 'Percentage Scanned from Cache' metric at 95%. What does this indicate?

options.A: 95% of the query's result was served from the result cache in Cloud Services
options.B: 95% of the data read by the warehouse came from the local SSD cache rather than remote storage
options.C: 95% of the micro-partitions were pruned and not scanned
options.D: 95% of the query execution time was spent in the Cloud Services layer

explanation: 'Percentage Scanned from Cache' in the Query Profile refers to the warehouse's local SSD cache hit rate — 95% of data bytes were read from local SSD rather than fetched from remote cloud storage. This is the warehouse cache, not the result cache (which would skip execution entirely) and not pruning (shown separately).

text: A table has a clustering key on ORDER_DATE. SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION reports an average clustering depth of 1.2. What does this mean?

options.A: The table is very poorly clustered and needs re-clustering
options.B: The table is well-clustered — a depth close to 1 means minimal overlap between micro-partition ranges
options.C: 1.2 indicates the table has 1.2 million micro-partitions
options.D: The clustering key is invalid because depth must be exactly 1.0

explanation: Average clustering depth near 1.0 indicates well-clustered data with minimal overlap in micro-partition ranges for the clustering key columns. Higher depth means more overlap and worse pruning efficiency. A depth of 1.2 is excellent. There is no requirement for depth to be exactly 1.0.

text: A query groups 100 million rows by CUSTOMER_ID and calculates COUNT(*) per group. The engineer wants an approximate result with faster performance. Which function should replace the exact COUNT?

options.A: HLL(CUSTOMER_ID)
options.B: APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT(CUSTOMER_ID)
options.C: There is no approximate alternative for grouped COUNT(*) — approximation only works for DISTINCT counts
options.D: SAMPLE(100000) on the source table before grouping

explanation: Snowflake's approximate functions (HLL, APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT) estimate the number of DISTINCT values, not row counts per group. There is no approximate version of COUNT(*) in Snowflake. To speed up grouped aggregation, consider materialized views, clustering, or larger warehouses. SAMPLE reduces input rows but produces statistically different results.

text: An engineer writes a query: SELECT * FROM events WHERE event_data:user_agent::VARCHAR ILIKE '%mobile%'. The VARIANT column event_data is not cast to a typed column. Can the Search Optimization Service (SOS) accelerate this query?

options.A: No — SOS does not support queries on VARIANT paths
options.B: Yes — SOS supports equality and substring searches on VARIANT fields including ILIKE patterns
options.C: Yes — but only if the VARIANT field is first extracted into a virtual column
options.D: No — SOS only accelerates equality predicates, not ILIKE/LIKE patterns

explanation: SOS supports search optimization on VARIANT fields, including equality predicates and substring searches (LIKE/ILIKE). It creates search access paths for semi-structured data paths without requiring extraction into separate columns. SOS handles both structured and semi-structured column optimization.

text: A table's VARIANT column stores arrays. The engineer needs to explode each array element into its own row while preserving the original row's columns. Which SQL construct achieves this?

options.A: UNPIVOT on the VARIANT column
options.B: LATERAL FLATTEN on the VARIANT column with an OUTER => FALSE join
options.C: SPLIT_TO_TABLE on the VARIANT column
options.D: ARRAY_SLICE to extract individual elements in a loop

explanation: LATERAL FLATTEN expands array/object elements into rows and is joined laterally with the source row, preserving original columns. OUTER => FALSE (default) excludes rows with empty arrays. UNPIVOT works on relational columns, not VARIANT arrays. SPLIT_TO_TABLE splits strings. There is no loop construct in Snowflake SQL.

text: Which TWO conditions INVALIDATE the result cache for a previously cached query? (Select TWO)

options.A: A DML operation (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) modifies the underlying table
options.B: A different user runs the exact same query text
options.C: The USE_CACHED_RESULT session parameter is set to FALSE
options.D: More than 24 hours have passed since the result was cached
options.E: The warehouse used for the original query is suspended

explanation: Result cache is invalidated when: (1) underlying data changes via DML, or (2) 24 hours have elapsed. Different users CAN use each other's cached results if roles allow. USE_CACHED_RESULT = FALSE prevents using cache but doesn't invalidate it. Warehouse suspension does not affect result cache — it lives in Cloud Services.

text: An analyst runs SELECT * FROM orders WHERE event_data:status::VARCHAR = 'shipped'. The event_data column is VARIANT. A colleague claims this query will always result in an error because you cannot compare VARIANT fields to strings. Is the colleague correct?

options.A: Yes — VARIANT fields cannot be compared to string literals
options.B: No — the explicit ::VARCHAR cast converts the VARIANT element to a string, and the equality comparison works correctly
options.C: No — but only because ILIKE is used instead of a strict equality
options.D: Yes — you must use GET_PATH() instead of colon notation for comparisons

explanation: VARIANT elements accessed via colon notation return VARIANT values. Explicitly casting with ::VARCHAR converts the element to a string, allowing standard equality comparisons. Without the cast, comparing a VARIANT to a string literal still works in many cases due to implicit casting, but explicit casting is the recommended and reliable approach.

text: A JSON object is stored in a VARIANT column: {"status": "1"}. An analyst runs WHERE col:status = 1. The query returns zero rows, even though the row exists. Why?

options.A: Colon notation does not work for top-level keys in VARIANT columns
options.B: The JSON value '1' is a string, but the comparison is to integer 1 — VARIANT preserves the JSON type, so string '1' does not equal integer 1
options.C: The column must be cast to OBJECT before path access works
options.D: The WHERE clause cannot reference VARIANT columns directly

explanation: VARIANT preserves JSON types. The value '1' in JSON is a string (wrapped in quotes), while 1 without quotes is a number. Comparing string-VARIANT to integer 1 returns FALSE. The fix is either: WHERE col:status = '1' (compare as string) or WHERE col:status::INTEGER = 1 (cast to integer). This is a common exam trap — JSON type awareness matters.

text: A provider wants to share data with a consumer in a different cloud region (provider is AWS US-East, consumer is Azure West Europe). Direct sharing is not possible. What is the recommended approach?

options.A: Create a replication group to replicate data to the consumer's region first
options.B: Publish the data as a listing, which supports cross-region access via Auto-Fulfillment
options.C: Export the data to a cloud storage bucket accessible by both regions
options.D: Ask the consumer to create an account in the provider's region

explanation: Direct shares are limited to the same region. Listings (via Snowflake Marketplace or private listings) support cross-region and cross-cloud delivery using Auto-Fulfillment, which automatically replicates data to the consumer's region. This is the recommended approach for cross-region sharing.

text: A consumer creates TABLE local_copy AS SELECT * FROM shared_db.schema.shared_table. The consumer then runs INSERT INTO local_copy VALUES (...). What is the result?

options.A: The INSERT fails because data originating from a share is always read-only
options.B: The INSERT succeeds — CTAS creates an independent writable copy in the consumer's storage
options.C: The INSERT succeeds but the new rows are visible to the provider
options.D: The INSERT fails because the consumer does not have OWNERSHIP on shared data

explanation: CREATE TABLE AS SELECT from a shared object creates a fully independent, writable copy in the consumer's own storage. This table is no longer linked to the share — the consumer has full OWNERSHIP and can INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE freely. The provider cannot see the consumer's local copy.

text: A Reader Account has been created by a provider. The Reader Account administrator creates a warehouse and runs analytical queries. Who is billed for the compute credits consumed?

options.A: The Reader Account is billed separately with its own contract
options.B: The provider who created the Reader Account pays for all compute
options.C: Snowflake absorbs the cost as part of the sharing service
options.D: The consumer pays only if they exceed a monthly free tier

explanation: Reader Accounts are created and managed by the provider. ALL compute costs (warehouse credits) in a Reader Account are billed to the provider's account. Reader Accounts do not have their own billing relationship with Snowflake. This is why providers should carefully manage Reader Account warehouse sizes and access.

text: A Native App is installed by a consumer from the Snowflake Marketplace. The app includes custom stored procedures and UI components. Where does the app's compute run?

options.A: On the provider's dedicated application compute cluster
options.B: On the consumer's compute resources within their own account
options.C: On a shared Snowflake-managed pool split between provider and consumer
options.D: On the Marketplace infrastructure maintained by Snowflake

explanation: Native Apps run on the consumer's compute resources within their account. The provider packages code + data, but execution happens on the consumer side. This is a key difference from Reader Accounts (where the provider pays for compute). Application roles defined by the provider control access within the consumer's account.

text: An engineer runs UNDROP TABLE on a table that was dropped 3 days ago. The table was a permanent table with DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 1 on Enterprise Edition. Can the table be recovered?

options.A: Yes — UNDROP uses Fail-safe, which retains permanent table data for 7 days
options.B: No — the table's Time Travel retention expired after 1 day, and UNDROP operates within the Time Travel window only
options.C: Yes — Enterprise Edition always provides 90 days of recovery regardless of the retention setting
options.D: No — UNDROP only works for transient and temporary tables

explanation: UNDROP operates within the Time Travel retention window. If retention is set to 1 day and 3 days have passed, the table is beyond the Time Travel window and cannot be recovered with UNDROP. Fail-safe is NOT user-accessible — only Snowflake Support can attempt recovery from Fail-safe. Enterprise Edition allows UP TO 90 days, but the actual setting was 1 day.
